##Test ID
AAFUNCTION.1
##Description
Create a PDS4 Product Label using a design tool based on PDS’s schema.

##Requirements
L5.PRP.DE.*

##Success Criteria
Design tool produces a syntactically valid PDS Product Label else indicates where the label is invalid.

##Test Steps
In general:
• Consult Append D of the Data Providers’ Handbook (DPH), Version 0.3.10

##Test Results
Creation of a label-template (xml) from the master-schema (xsd).

##Comments
Results met test successful criteria

##Date of Testing
2013.04.08

##Test Personnel
Richard Chen

##Test ID
AAFUNCTION.2
##Description
Validate PDS4 label

##Requirements
L5.PRP.VA.1, L5.PRP.VA.2, L5.PRP.VA.5, L5.PRP.VA.6, L5.PRP.VA.7, L5.PRP.VA.9

##Success Criteria
Validation tool validates a file or all eligible products in a directory tree, indicates the schemas utilized during the validation, and ensures that a product label is well formed XML and conforms to its schemas. Also validate for content as well as syntax.

##Test Steps
1. cd testDir/context_bundle_20130326
2. validate . -x PDS4_PDS_0310b.xsd -S PDS4_PDS_0310b.sch -e "*.xml"

##Date of Testing
2013.04.09

##Test Personnel
Richard Chen

##Test ID
AAFUNCTION.4
##Description
Search for PDS4 data.

##Requirements
L5.SCH.1, L5.SCH.5, L5.SCH.6, L5.SCH.8, L5.SCH.10, L5.SCH.11, L5.SCH.12

##Success Criteria
After configuration (e.g. regenerating search indices), Search returns the data harvested in the previous step.

##Test Steps
Build the search index
1. In a browser, http://localhost:8080/search-ui
2. Beneath “Data Search” in the middle of the page, type “cassini”
3. search-core -d -H binDir/search-service-1.2.0/pds -r http://localhost:8080/registry >& s1.out
   This may be slow. binDir/search-service-1.2.0/pds should grow constantly.
4. cd binDir/search-core-1.2.0/bin
5. solr-post binDir/search-service-1.2.0/pds http://localhost:8080/search-service/pds > testDir/s2.out
6. Repeat steps 1 and 2

##Test Results
Step 2:

The previous test (AAFUNCTION.3) harvests/registers a bundle, but the search index has not been built. That's why step 2 shows that searching for "cassini" gives 0 results. Steps 3 and 5 below build the index, causing the search now succeeds.

Step 3 writes much output to stderr, captured in s1.out, which has:

---- RUNNING IN DEBUG MODE ----

Apr 9, 2013 1:12:18 PM gov.nasa.pds.search.core.extractor.RegistryExtractor createOutputs
INFO: Create registry dir: /PDS4tools/search-service-1.2.0/pds/registry-data
Apr 9, 2013 1:12:18 PM gov.nasa.pds.search.core.SearchCoreLauncher runRegistryExtractor
INFO:
Registry URL: http://localhost:8080/registry
Search Home: /PDS4tools/search-service-1.2.0/pds
Config Home: /PDS4tools/search-core-1.2.0/conf/pds/pds3
Apr 9, 2013 1:12:18 PM gov.nasa.pds.search.core.extractor.RegistryExtractor

run
INFO: ----- Beginning Registry Data Extraction -----
...

Step 5 writes much to stdout, captured in s2.out, which has:

Posting index to SOLR

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<response>
<lst name="responseHeader"><int name="status">0</int><int name="QTime">130</int></lst>
</response>
Backing up '/PDS4tools/search-service-1.2.0/pds/data' to '/PDS4tools/search-service-1.2.0/pds/data_old'
Running XSLT transformation...
/PDS4tools/search-service-1.2.0/pds
...

Step 6:

The first image shows the PDS “Data Search” page immediately after Step 6 begins. The browser is at http://localhost:8080/search-ui/. The user has entered a query (cassini cda) into the search field, but no results are yet displayed. This represents the starting state of the user interface after the search index has been rebuilt.

The second image shows the result of executing that same search after the index build has completed. The browser has navigated to the results page (/search-ui/search.jsp?q=cassini). The page now displays a populated “Search Results” view:

A results summary appears (e.g., “1–50 of 185 results”).

The left sidebar shows refinement facets (Type, Target, Investigation, etc.) with counts.

The main content area lists “Search Tools” and “Data Sets and Information” related to “cassini.”

Individual result entries (e.g., Cassini Image Search, OPUS, Cassini RSS data sets, targets) are visible and clickable.

In other words, Step 6 demonstrates the transition from an empty or pre-search state (first image) to a fully populated search results page (second image). This confirms that, after building the search index in the earlier steps, entering the same query now returns real, structured PDS data instead of zero results.

##Comments
Results met test successful criteria

Notes:
search-core may fail if testbed does not have enough memory with following message near the bottom of $CATALINA_HOME/logs/catalina.out
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space

There is no issue with testbed that has sufficient memory. The following workaround were implemented:

in .cshrc
• setenv JAVA_OPTS "-Xmx256m"

Another way to reduce

memory demands is to reduce the size of the harvested files. Flush the database, then before harvest and search-core, for each context_dataset file:
• testDir/bin/chopMiddle.py -a "<data_set_desc>" -b "</data_set_desc>" -c "shortened" -O context_dataset/Product_20130322/eachFile.xml

testDir/bin/chopMiddle.py -a "<confidence_level_note>" -b "</confidence_level_note>" -c "short2" -O context_dataset/Product_20130322/eachFile.xml

##Date of Testing
2013.04.09

##Test Personnel
Richard Chen

##Test ID
AATESTME.1
##Description
Query for a registered artifact

##Requirements
L5.REG.14

##Success Criteria
If artifact was previously registered, return a positive message. If not, negative.

##Test Steps
Verify in browser: http://localhost:8080/registry-ui
If “Product Registry” shows rows of data, type “urn:nasa:pds:TESTING*” in LID, then hit “Update”, or run RESETREGISTRY in Section 3.1
If registry-ui is not installed,
http://localhost:8080/registry/extrinsics/logicals/urn:nasa:pds:TESTING:example.DPH.sampleProducts:exampleProducts:Array2D_Image.MPFL-M-IMP-2-EDR-V1.0

##Test Results
The image shows a web browser open to http://localhost:8080/registry-ui/ displaying the "Registry Service" interface of the Planetary Data System. The page header reads "Planetary Data System – Registry Service" and includes a banner image of planets.

Below the header is a tabbed interface with tabs labeled: Products, Associations, Packages, Services, Events, Schemes, and Classification Nodes. The "Products" tab is selected.

The main panel contains filter fields labeled GUID, LID, Name, Object Type (with a dropdown showing "Any Object Type"), and Status (with a dropdown showing "Any Status").

Under these filters is a section titled "Product Registry" with column headers such as Name, LID, Version Name, Object Type, and Status.

The table area displays the message "There is no data to display", indicating that no matching registry entries are currently shown. This visual confirms the state of the registry UI before or after performing the query steps, and demonstrates the interface where a user would enter a LID such as "urn:nasa:pds:TESTING*" and update the view to retrieve registered artifacts.

##Comments
Results met test successful criteria

##Test ID
AATESTME.3
##Description
For Harvest, provide a command-line interface, accept a configuration file, determine candidates for registration, capture metadata, submit metadata to the Registry Service. Registry accepts the artifact, assigns a global unique ID to the product. Registry relates artifacts via (LID-based) association.

##Requirements
L5.HVT.1, L5.HVT.2, L5.HVT.5, L5.HVT.6, L5.HVT.7, L5.REG.1, L5.REG.2, L5.REG.4, L5.REG.6, L5.REG.8, L5.SEC.1

##Success Criteria
Harvest tool, executed from the command line, discovers all matching artifacts and for each submits metadata, based on both identifying and artifact-specific metadata, to the Registry service. A matching artifact matches the criteria given in the user-edited configuration file. Tools to view the registry should show the matching artifacts, with appropriate metadata, including the guid, which is assigned by the Registry. Tools to view the registry show the association.

##Test Steps
1. Create the data file referred to in the label:
   touch holdings/i943630r.raw
2. Register holdings/minimalImage.xml, as its <Product_Observational> matches one of harvest-policy-master.xml’s <candidates><productMetadata>:
   harvest testDir/holdings -c harvest-policy-master.xml
3. Verify the product registration: http://localhost:8080/registry-ui
4. Verify the registration of LID-based associations:
   http://localhost:8080/registry/extrinsics/logicals/urn:nasa:pds:TESTING:example.DPH.sampleProducts:exampleProducts:Array2D_Image.MPFL-M-IMP-2-EDR-V1.0

##Description
Query and delete associations (not just products). When Harvest registers a product, it also registers associations to the XML label file and to the referenced data file. These associations (non-LID-based) can be deleted.

L5.REG.13, L5.REG.14

##Success Criteria
The associations are removed from the registry.

##Test Steps
1. Clean database as described in RESETREGISTRY in Section 3.1
2. As in AATESTME.3’s step 2:
   harvest testDir/holdings -c harvest-policy-master.xml
3. Step 2’s output includes the GUID of the associations between the data product and 1) the label and 2) the data file. Replace guid below with the GUIDs:
   http://localhost:8080/registry/associations/guid
4. Delete one of the associations:
   curl -X DELETE -v http://localhost:8080/registry/associations/guid
5. Repeat step 3.
6. Another way to delete an association is to delete its source object. Delete the product via its GUID:
   curl -v -X DELETE http://localhost:8080/registry/extrinsics/guid
7. Repeat step 3. Both associations should be gone.

##Test Steps
The catalog ingest requires the registry, transport, and storage services be configured together, which is likely too complex for local testing. So before testing:

• The registry resides at http://localhost:8080 (edit script “catalog” to alter)
• The administrator of the registry provides:
  o a keystore (in this example, the file is /somewhere/keystore)
  o a keystore password (e.g. hellopds)
  o URL for the registry (https://potato.registry.jpl.nasa.gov)
  o URL for the storage service (http://potato.jpl.nasa.gov:9100)
  o URL for the transport serv (http://potato.jpl.nasa.gov:80/cas-product)
  o username/password for the security service (rchen/new4rchen)
• The keystore has been copied/moved to /somewhere/keystore
• In the local script catalog (or catalog.bat), the java command has parameters
  o -Dpds.registry="https://potato.registry.jpl.nasa.gov/registry"
  o -Dpds.security.keystore="/somewhere/keystore"
• To simplify the command-line, create a configuration file named config:

citool.mode        = ingest
citool.user        = rchen
citool.pass        = new4rchen
citool.serverUrl   = http://potato.jpl.nasa.gov:9100
citool.transportUrl = http://potato.jpl.nasa.gov:9000
citool.keypass     = pdscat
citool.verbose     = 1

1. catalog -ctestCatalog/config -t testCatalog/CORPWS_0164/ -r ctlg1.out

See reasonable results based on text such as “Cassini”.

##Test Steps
Test AAFUNCTION.4 populates the PDS4 database with PDS3 information. Compare the PDS4 (http://localhost:8080/search-ui) and the PDS3 (http://pds.nasa.gov/tools/data-search-pds3) results for the following searches:
1. mro spice
2. cassini cda
3. voyager plasma wave. In Refine Your Search, click “Comet SL9/Jupiter Collision”

5. neptune
6. jupiter images
7. corona
8. NEAR-A-SPICE-6-V1.0 (a specific data set ID)

##Test Results
All searches complete successfully. In general, PDS4 returns many more results than PDS3, which is by design. “Good” and “Bad” refer to PDS4 relative to PDS3:
1. bad: first result is “Search Tool: Comet Data Searcher” instead of the data set.
2. bad: no Cassini High Rate Detector dataset, which is related
3. good: no “Search Tool: Comet Data Searcher”.
4. Apparently, “digital elevation map” is not a commonly used term. Both have mostly irrelevant results
   bad: PDS3 has the 2 best results, datasets “VO1/VO2 MARS VISUAL IMAGING SUBSYSTEM DIGITAL TERRAIN MODEL” and “MRO-M-HIRISE-5-DTM-V1.0”. PDS4 does not have those at all.
5. good: fewer extraneous datasets, e.g. NH-X-*
   good: nine extra instruments, seemingly relevant
6. bad: 13 extra, irrelevant search tools
   good: extra target GEOGRAPHOS
7. good: 3 missions SUISEI, VEX, ULY.
   good: far fewer probably irrelevant datasets
8. PDS3 returns 1 result. PDS4 returns 2703. PDS3’s result is probably better.

Results met successful test criteria.
